header: Head of B2B Unit / Quinto Andar

lesson.menu_title: Q&A Under Fire -- Thinking on Your Feet
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Q&A Under Fire -- Thinking on Your Feet
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eduardo Chiba | Aula 13 -- Q&A Under Fire

listenings.text: So, after my presentation, an investor put me on the spot. He asked, in a sharp tone, why our churn was so high compared with the market. Honestly, I did not have the exact figure off the top of my head. So I bought myself a little time. I said, that is a fair question, and let me make sure I understand it correctly. Are you asking about churn this quarter, or over the full year? He clarified it was the full year. Then I hedged a bit, because I was not a hundred percent sure of the number. I said, I would say it is roughly eight percent, but let me confirm the exact figure and get back to you after the session. The key was this: I did not freeze, and I did not invent a number. I acknowledged the question, I asked for clarification, and I stayed calm under fire.

Let me think about how I handle tough questions in the Q&A. My old habit was to answer instantly, even when I was not sure. That is how you give a wrong number in front of the board. So here is my new rule for thinking on my feet. First, I buy time with a phrase, something like, that is a great question, let me think for a second. Second, if the question is not clear, I rephrase it back, so I answer the right thing. Third, when I am not certain, I hedge. I say roughly, or it is hard to say exactly, but. And finally, if I really do not know, I am honest. I say, I do not have that figure right now, but I will get back to you. Calm beats fast. A thoughtful pause sounds far more confident than a rushed, wrong answer.

extra_audio.text: First, you acknowledge the question, so the person feels heard. Then you buy yourself a little time with a short phrase. After that, you rephrase the question if it is not completely clear. Next, you give your answer, and you hedge it if you are not fully sure. Finally, if you do not have the figure, you promise to get back to them.
